one_line_takeaway: ISTS defends text-to-image watermarks against removal and forgery attacks by making the watermark's injection time and location instance-specific (via a CLIP-feature-based selector) and by using a two-sided detection statistic, yielding the best average and worst-case AUC/TPR@1%FPR across three prior removal and three prior forgery attacks compared to seven baseline watermarking schemes, though worst-case robustness against the strongest individual attacks is still incomplete.
key_finding: Against removal attacks (Table 1), ISTS achieves the best average (AUC 0.9363 / TPR@1%FPR 0.58) and worst-case (AUC 0.8210 / TPR@1%FPR 0.18) robustness of all 8 methods compared. Its largest margin is against Imp-Removal, where the strongest prior baseline (Shallow Diffuse) reaches only AUC 0.6752/TPR 0.00, while ISTS reaches AUC 0.8210/TPR 0.18 -- described in the paper as over a 20% AUC improvement and an 18-fold TPR@1%FPR improvement. Against forgery attacks (Table 2, where lower AUC/TPR is better/more robust), ISTS attains the lowest average (AUC 0.6856 / TPR 0.12) and lowest worst-case (AUC 0.9491 / TPR 0.37) scores among the 8 methods, with the strongest gain on Imp-Forgery: strongest baseline Shallow Diffuse at AUC 0.7621 vs. ISTS at AUC 0.6340 (paper states >20% AUC improvement). On non-adversarial image distortions (Table 3), ISTS is comparable to but slightly below the best performers Tree-Ring and RingID (average AUC 0.9742 vs. RingID's 0.9874; worst-case AUC 0.9331 vs. RingID's 0.9526), and it is noted that RingID's strong distortion-robustness does not carry over to removal/forgery robustness, where RingID performs poorly (e.g., AUC 0.0121 against Imp-Removal, Table 1). The ablation (Table 4) shows dynamic pattern selection is the dominant driver of forgery robustness (removing it drops Imp-Forgery AUC from 0.6340 to 0.7202, i.e. worse/higher, and Avg-Forgery from 0.4737 to 0.6014), while two-sided detection is the dominant driver of removal robustness against Imp-Removal specifically (dropping from AUC 0.8210 to 0.7129 when reverted to one-sided detection).
core_contribution: The paper proposes ISTS (Instance-Specific watermarking with Two-Sided detection), a new content-watermarking scheme for text-to-image diffusion models designed specifically to resist removal and forgery attacks. It has two components, both original to this paper: (1) instance-specific dynamic watermarking, where the injection timestep t and the Fourier-space pattern location l are chosen per-generation by a semantic-based selector (a classifier trained via K-Means clustering on CLIP features of non-watermarked images), rather than using a single static pattern/location as in Tree-Ring and its successors; and (2) two-sided detection, which replaces the standard one-sided distance metric d = mean|W_i - F(z_T)_i| with a min of the one-sided distance and its sign-flipped counterpart, d = min(mean|W_i - F(z_T)_i|, mean|W_i + F(z_T)_i|), to close a vulnerability where an adversary can drive the watermarked latent toward the opposite sign to evade one-sided detection.
threat_model: Adversary goals: (a) removal attacks -- given a watermarked image I_w, find a small perturbation delta (bounded by a similarity metric d(I_w+delta, I_w) <= epsilon) such that the detector D outputs 0 (non-watermarked); (b) forgery attacks -- given a benign (non-watermarked) image I_b, find delta such that D(I_b+delta)=1 while preserving perceptual similarity to I_b. Adversary knowledge/capability: no access to the target diffusion model's weights and no knowledge of the specific watermarking algorithm or implementation, but the adversary can use surrogate diffusion models to compute gradients (enabling gradient-based attacks), and may possess one or more leaked watermarked reference images, or a set of watermarked and non-watermarked images from which to extract population-level statistics (e.g., by averaging). This is the threat model used in the three attack families the paper evaluates against: Muller et al.'s gradient-based imprinting attack (Imp-Removal/Forgery), Yang et al.'s averaging attack (Avg-Removal/Forgery), and Jain et al.'s VAE-encoder attack (VAE-Removal/Forgery) -- all cited prior work, not introduced by this paper.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitation: worst-case robustness remains 'not satisfactory' against the strongest specific attacks -- Imp-Removal (worst-case AUC 0.8210, well below the near-1.0 AUC achieved in non-adversarial settings) and VAE-Forgery (worst-case AUC 0.9491, i.e. still fairly easy to forge). Additional limitations evident from the paper's own tables/text (not merely author-claimed): ISTS is not the best method on every axis -- it is outperformed by SEAL on VAE-Forgery specifically, by ROBIN on plain image quality (PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS, comparable rather than superior), and by Tree-Ring/RingID on average non-adversarial distortion robustness. The evaluation set for adversarial attacks is modest (100 image pairs) and uses a single base model (SD-2.1-base) and a single prompt source, so generalization to other diffusion backbones or prompt distributions is untested in this paper. The threat model itself assumes the adversary lacks knowledge of the watermarking scheme's internals (single-shot, black-box-ish attacks with a surrogate model); the paper does not evaluate an adaptive adversary who knows ISTS's dynamic-selection mechanism and attempts to defeat it directly (e.g., by first inferring or approximating the semantic-based selector).

datasets_used: Prompt set following prior work (Wen et al./Tree-Ring and Arabi et al./SEAL) for text-to-image generation
100 watermarked/non-watermarked image pairs for removal and forgery attack evaluation (following Muller et al.'s setup)
quantitative_results: Removal, Imp-Removal: ISTS AUC 0.8210/TPR 0.18 vs. best baseline Shallow Diffuse AUC 0.6752/TPR 0.00 (Table 1)
Removal, average across 3 attacks: ISTS AUC 0.9363/TPR 0.58, best of all 8 methods (Table 1)
Removal, worst-case across 3 attacks: ISTS AUC 0.8210/TPR 0.18, best of all 8 methods (Table 1)
Forgery, Imp-Forgery: ISTS AUC 0.6340/TPR 0.00 vs. best baseline Shallow Diffuse AUC 0.7621/TPR 0.11 (Table 2, lower=better)
Forgery, average across 3 attacks: ISTS AUC 0.6856/TPR 0.12, lowest of all 8 methods (Table 2)
Forgery, worst-case across 3 attacks: ISTS AUC 0.9491/TPR 0.37, lowest of all 8 methods (Table 2)
Non-adversarial distortion robustness (Table 3): ISTS average AUC 0.9742, worst-case AUC 0.9331, vs. RingID average 0.9874 / worst-case 0.9526 (best of comparison set)

Ablation (Table 4): removing dynamic pattern raises Imp-Forgery AUC from 0.6340 to 0.7202 and Avg-Forgery from 0.4737 to 0.6014 (worse robustness); removing two-sided detection lowers Imp-Removal AUC from 0.8210 to 0.7129 (worse robustness)
